### Post by ministoat on 2008-07-16
my fstab needs a little bit of sorting after i cleared up a lot of messy paritioning (imo!)..but i'm a noob at this so thought I'd ask for help in case I bork my install..

here's the current fstab:

# /etc/fstab: static file system information.
#
#  -- This file has been automaticly generated by ntfs-config -- 
#
# <file system> <mount point>   <type>  <options>       <dump>  <pass>

proc /proc proc defaults 0 0
# Entry for /dev/sdb5 :
UUID=fbd1f315-e066-473e-8607-837c3f131879 / ext3 relatime,errors=remount-ro 0 1
# Entry for /dev/sdb6 :
UUID=98a8b029-6f23-4d59-825a-ab6239c2c47d none swap sw 0 0
/dev/scd1 /media/cdrom0 udf,iso9660 user,noauto,exec,utf8 0 0
/dev/scd0 /media/cdrom1 udf,iso9660 user,noauto,exec,utf8 0 0
/dev/fd0 /media/floppy0 auto rw,user,noauto,exec,utf8 0 0
/dev/sdb1 /media/spinpoint ntfs-3g defaults,locale=en_GB.UTF-8 0 0
/dev/sda1 /media/Music ntfs-3g force 0 0
/dev/sdb2 /media/Windows ntfs-3g force 0 0




Now I've removed most of the ntfs partitions, and found that since making a new ext3 partition I can't access it..probably due to something in there..I tried doing a "/dev/disk/by-label -lah" but the command found no such file.

---

### Post by dracayr on 2008-07-16
you can delete the lines of the partitions you deleted. then, make a new line like this:

/dev/*ext3partition name*  *mount point* ext3 defaults 0 1


dracayr

---

### Post by ministoat on 2008-07-16
thanks for the reply..I don't think the new partition has a name other than "160gb media"..shall i just use this?

---

### Post by dracayr on 2008-07-16
no. each partition has a name like sda*some number*. Your partition will probably be sda1, sdb1,sdb2,sdb3 or sda2,depending on wether you deleted the ntfs partitions first or created the new partition first, and on which hd you created it. After you made the changes, you have to reboot.

dracayr

---

### Post by bodhi.zazen on 2008-07-16
IMO you should mount your partitions by uuid.

UUID=xxx.yyy.zzz /media/mount_point ......

To see your partitions by UUID:

```
sudo blkid
```

[How to fstab - Ubuntu Forums]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?&t=283131")

---

### Post by dracayr on 2008-07-16
bodhi,can you explain why? I know that a UUID is a number that refers to a partition, but why is it better than device names?

dracayr

---

### Post by bodhi.zazen on 2008-07-16
"Better" may be a bit of an over statement. /dev/sdxy adn uuid each has advantages and disadvantages.

partitions can change, especially with removable devices, while UUID only change if you re-format.

Either is fine, UUID is, IMHO, very easy, you can copy and paste the UUID from your terminal after listing it with blkid.

I found UUID intimidating at first, but now it seems very intuitive. I use UUID for all partitions rather then /dev/sdxy for internal drives and UUID for removable devices/media.

---

thanks for the replies :)

### Post by bodhi.zazen on 2008-07-19
The entries you added are off. See this thread :

[How to fstab - Ubuntu Forums]("http://ubuntuforums.org/showthread.php?&t=283131") 

There is a section there as to how to label as well.

### Post by ministoat on 2008-07-31
ok..i've been finding this hard to get my head around, sorry; how's this?



# Entry for /dev/sdb5 :
UUID=fbd1f315-e066-473e-8607-837c3f131879 / ext3 relatime,errors=remount-ro 0 1

# Entry for /dev/sdb6 :
UUID=98a8b029-6f23-4d59-825a-ab6239c2c47d none swap sw 0 0

# Entry for Second Hard Drive /dev/sda1 :
UUID="00d29356-f908-4eb0-ba0a-e3932e07c619" /mnt/ext3 ext3 auto,ministoat 0 0

#Entry for Windows Partition /dev/sdb1 :
UUID="1BC54AD655D24406" /mnt/Vista  ntfs auto,ministoat 0 0




/dev/scd1 /media/cdrom0 udf,iso9660 user,noauto,exec,utf8 0 0
/dev/scd0 /media/cdrom1 udf,iso9660 user,noauto,exec,utf8 0 0
/dev/fd0 /media/floppy0 auto rw,user,noauto,exec,utf8 0 0

### Post by sisco311 on 2008-07-31
try:
>  # Entry for Second Hard Drive /dev/sda1 :
UUID=00d29356-f908-4eb0-ba0a-e3932e07c619 /mnt/ext3 ext3 defaults 0 2

#Entry for Windows Partition /dev/sdb1 :
UUID=1BC54AD655D24406 /mnt/Vista  ntfs defaults,umask=007,gid=46 0 0


---

### Post by ministoat on 2008-07-31
will that still give me rw permissions on both?

thanks for the reply :)

---

### Post by sisco311 on 2008-07-31
yes.
read/write/execute permission on the ntfs partition.

after the ext3 partition is mounted change the owner of the partition:
```
sudo chown -R ministoat:ministoat /mnt/ex3
```assuming that ministoat is your login name.
[https://help.ubuntu.com/community/FilePermissions](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/FilePermissions)

---
